modules.module: M1: Why Side Projects Stall and How Agile Fixes It

Claude.notes: Marcus, Priya, Jordan, and Amara each open a lesson by living the exact failure the concept then dissects. Story-to-concept linkage is unusually tight. The LIRA stack is a clean, original framework. Minor ding: the text occasionally tells the insight rather than letting the protagonist arrive at it.
Claude.topIssue: A few transitions explicitly summarize 'what's worth noticing' rather than letting the reader land the insight from the narrative.

Gemini.notes: Agile Manifesto attribution (2001, four preferences) is accurate. Eric Ries / Lean Startup (2011) reference is correct. However, 'premature scaling' is used here in a non-standard way — Steve Blank's term refers to scaling growth before product-market fit, not building infrastructure before validation. Sprint, backlog, velocity definitions are appropriately calibrated.
Gemini.topIssue: The term 'premature scaling' is redefined to mean 'building infrastructure for an unvalidated product'; the established product-development meaning (scaling before PMF) is different.

Perplexity.notes: Tools cited (Cursor, Replit Agent, Lovable, Bolt.new, Claude) are still front-of-mind for solo builders in 2026. The 'speed of building vs. speed of learning' frame holds up well in a year where AI scaffolding is commodified. Capability delta is unusually concrete: a learner can run a one-week solo sprint by Friday after this module.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 2024–2025 dating in the narrative reads slightly nostalgic from May 2026; a single line acknowledging continued tool churn would future-proof the framing.

modules.module: M2: From Vague Idea to Real Customer

Claude.notes: The Customer Specificity Ladder is the strongest single framework in the course — five rungs from demographic to named person, each demonstrably testable. Marcus (UCSD) and Devon's contrasting feedback sessions create the exact problem the lesson then solves. Character-name reuse across modules (Marcus, Priya) is mildly confusing for sequential readers.
Claude.topIssue: Marcus and Priya are reintroduced as different people from Module 1; a reader proceeding linearly will briefly stumble before realizing these are distinct case characters.

Gemini.notes: Five Whys is correctly attributed to manufacturing quality control (Toyota Production System lineage, though Toyota isn't named). User Story / Acceptance Criteria definitions are consistent with mainstream agile usage. The Symptom-vs-Root-Problem distinction is well-handled and the 'symptom-level products can work' acknowledgment is technically honest.
Gemini.topIssue: Five Whys would benefit from naming Sakichi Toyoda / Toyota explicitly; current attribution to 'manufacturing quality control' is correct but vague.

Perplexity.notes: The 'AI as constraint generator, not idea expander' move is sharply current — it captures the shift in how serious 2025–2026 builders use Claude/ChatGPT for scoping rather than ideation. The 'could I call them this week' test still holds up as the cheapest viability signal available.
Perplexity.topIssue: The 'AI generates fifty backlog items in three minutes' caution is current but increasingly under-stated — 2026 reasoning models are even more eager to expand.

modules.module: M3: Solo Sprints and Vertical Slicing

Claude.notes: Marcus's 80-hours-in-fragments story creates a sharp contrast with Daniela's review-skipping shipper. Vertical slicing is the cleanest concept teaching in the course — recipe app example is concrete enough to copy. The 5-question sprint review template is immediately usable. Story-to-concept density slightly heavier than Advanced target but justified by the audience.
Claude.topIssue: Stories are excellent but the second half of L4 leans on 'compounding sprints' as the closing message; the shape of that argument tells more than it shows.

Gemini.notes: Vertical slicing is described accurately. 'Spike sprint' usage matches Scrum literature (time-boxed research/discovery). The estimation-bias point (software teams systematically over-optimistic) is consistent with research from McConnell and others, though a citation would strengthen it. 'Definition of Done' is used in a slightly stricter form than canonical Scrum, which is reasonable for solo work.
Gemini.topIssue: The estimation-research claim ('software estimation is grim, even experienced teams are systematically over-optimistic') is correct but uncited; a McConnell or Standish reference would harden the point.

Perplexity.notes: The recommendation that AI sprints explicitly budget a spike for 'will the model do this reliably?' is more relevant in 2026 than when the course was written — model-quality variance per use case has become the dominant scoping risk. Recovery protocol concept transfers well.
Perplexity.topIssue: M3 sets one-week sprints as default; in late-2025/2026 some solo builders are running daily 'micro-shipments' with agentic tooling — a brief acknowledgment would signal currency.

modules.module: M4: AI Research and Build Stacks

Claude.notes: Research Stack 'four jobs' framing is the load-bearing structure and it works. The Build Stack tool grid is helpful but reads more reference-card than narrative. Marcus's eleven-downloads opening is a tight problem-creating story; Priya's NIL-deals story is solid. Slightly less narrative density than M1–M3.
Claude.topIssue: The Build Stack tool grid (six tools, six descriptions) is informative but breaks the story-driven cadence — closer to a tools chapter in a reference guide than the rest of the course.

Gemini.notes: Tool-to-job mapping is broadly accurate as of 2024–2025 (Cursor for in-codebase work, Bolt.new for prompt-to-app, Copilot for in-editor completion, Claude for long-context architecture review). The 'steelman your competition' instruction is conceptually clean. Caveats about AI hallucinating competitor details are well-placed.
Gemini.topIssue: Specific model names (GPT-4o, ChatGPT o1/o3) and tool capability claims will need updating roughly every 6–9 months; the lesson would be more durable framed around tool *categories* (AI-native editor, prompt-to-app, long-context reviewer).

Perplexity.notes: This is the module most exposed to dating risk. By May 2026, 'GPT-4o' / 'ChatGPT o1/o3' phrasing reads dated — the live frontier has moved on, and several solo-builder workflows now include agentic CLI tools (Claude Code, Gemini CLI) that aren't mentioned. The research-job framework itself stays current; the tool-by-name mapping does not.
Perplexity.topIssue: Specific model references (GPT-4o, ChatGPT o1/o3) and missing 2025–2026 agentic CLI tooling make the Build Stack section the highest-priority refresh in the whole course.

modules.module: M5: Containing Scope with the Yes Machine

Claude.notes: 'Yes Machine' and 'Minimum Viable Boundary' are original framings that earn their keep — both make scope creep visible to the learner in a way standard MVP language doesn't. Maya's three-hour brainstorm story creates the problem cleanly. Parking-lot technique is well-introduced. The four creep-types (feature/audience/platform/quality) is a clean taxonomy.
Claude.topIssue: Lesson 1 lands the diagnosis ('AI is a yes machine') but Lesson 2 risks treating MVB as if it solves the underlying motivational pull toward expansion, which it only partially does.

Gemini.notes: The behavioral claim that LLMs are trained to be 'helpful, additive' is broadly consistent with RLHF-trained assistant behavior. Approach goals vs completion is correctly attributed to motivation research (though uncited). Definition of Done is used in a custom three-part form (user/action/binary test) which is a reasonable adaptation of Scrum's DoD.
Gemini.topIssue: The motivation-research claim about 'approach goals vs completion' is correct in spirit but uncited; one or two specific researcher names would lift this from anecdote to citation.

Perplexity.notes: The session-start re-anchoring prompt ('my MVB is X — flag suggestions that violate it') is exactly the discipline that maps to 2025–2026 chat habits, when context windows are larger but session memory drifts. The framing holds up well at current pace.
Perplexity.topIssue: Re-anchoring advice is solid but doesn't address persistent-memory features (some 2026 chat tools now retain user-defined constraints across sessions); a sentence on that would future-proof it.

modules.module: M6: Real Feedback from Real Users

Claude.notes: Marcus's 340-signups-but-11-returns story is the most dramatic problem-creator in the course — it forces the learner to confront that early metrics they'd celebrate are actually noise. Politeness loop, future-tense endorsement, three-sentence cold-outreach rule are all immediately usable. Priya's Saturday cold-outreach rescue is a strong counter-example.
Claude.topIssue: The 'three types of dangerous positive feedback' overlaps somewhat with the 'real validation' inverse list; a single integrated diagnostic table would tighten the mental model.

Gemini.notes: The 'compliment-shaped noise' phrase aligns with Rob Fitzpatrick's argument in The Mom Test (2013), which the course correctly cites in M1. Behavioral validation framing is consistent with mainstream startup-research literature. Past-behavior-vs-hypothetical questioning is well-grounded.
Gemini.topIssue: Fitzpatrick's name is given in M1 but should be re-cited here when his framework is the entire spine of M6 L1; a one-line attribution would close the loop.

Perplexity.notes: Cold-outreach platform inventory (Reddit, Discord, Twitter/X, Facebook Groups, IRL) is appropriately current for 2026 — and the explicit acknowledgment that Discord is replacing Facebook Groups for many niches reads accurate. Three-sentence outreach rule is durable advice.
Perplexity.topIssue: Twitter/X is mentioned as a research surface; in 2026 many target communities have migrated to Bluesky / Threads / niche fediverse instances, and the lesson should acknowledge that drift.

modules.module: M7: Documentation and the Hirable Portfolio

Claude.notes: Priya's 'It sounds like the AI did most of the work' interview moment is the sharpest problem-creating story in the course — every reader can immediately picture themselves in that chair. The Decision Log + Intellectual Ownership + AI Contribution Statement triad is a clean, modern documentation framework. Three-sentence portfolio formula is excellent.
Claude.topIssue: Lesson 2 introduces five portfolio elements *and* four platforms *and* three audiences; the cognitive load is highest in this module — could be split into 7a/7b.

Gemini.notes: Documentation framework matches what mature engineering orgs increasingly expect from candidates. The 'AI Contribution Statement' is a useful coined term and the lesson correctly notes it builds rather than undermines credibility — this is consistent with current hiring-research observations.
Gemini.topIssue: The five-component documentation set is substantially overlapping with arc42 / C4-style architecture-doc conventions but doesn't reference them; a one-line bridge would help technical readers.

Perplexity.notes: This is the most current module in the entire course. The 2025 hiring landscape — interviewers assuming AI use, evaluating *understanding* over authorship — is exactly what most mid-size SaaS teams now interview for. Loom-walkthrough advice is durable. Product Hunt / Substack / personal-site recommendations remain accurate.
Perplexity.topIssue: Product Hunt's gravity has shifted in 2026; supplementing it with 'Hacker News Show HN' and current AI-builder communities (e.g., Builder lounges on Discord) would keep the platform list fresh.

Claude.notes: Strong capstone. Maya's eleven-tabs midnight scene creates option-paralysis viscerally. The four-week sprint structure (Foundation → First User → Hardening + Second User → Launch) is the cleanest 30-day plan in the course. The pre-committed recovery protocol ('write the cuts on day 1, not day 19') is one of the most valuable practical takeaways in the whole curriculum.
Claude.topIssue: Dev's lost-five-days-to-spring-break story sets up recovery protocol well, but resolves with 'he eventually shipped three days late' rather than fully landing the protocol's value — a sharper resolution would tighten the close.

Gemini.notes: MLP (Minimum Launchable Product) is presented as distinct from MVP; this is essentially the author's coined refinement rather than an established product-development term. The reasoning — 'viable can mean unacceptably broken, launchable raises the bar' — is defensible but technically the distinction is bespoke.
Gemini.topIssue: MLP vs MVP: 'MLP' is not a widely recognized industry term. The lesson should either flag this as a course-specific framing or anchor it to closer established terms like 'demo-ready MVP' / 'Minimum Loveable Product' (Henrik Kniberg).

Perplexity.notes: 8–12 hour weekly capacity guidance for college-aged solo builders is empirically realistic. AI-as-scaffolding-not-judgment instruction is good advice in May 2026 when more capable agentic tools tempt builders to outsource the *interesting* decisions. Capstone week-by-week structure should remain useful through 2027.
Perplexity.topIssue: The hour-budget guidance (8–12 focused hours/week) is realistic but doesn't account for the 2026 reality that many builders now compress this into weekend agentic-AI bursts; one sentence on alternative cadences would strengthen it.
